summary: Clean soundboard recording with strong moments—standout Comes A Time and Drums‑Space—but muddy Midnight Hour and uneven playing make it a mixed‑quality show.
review: The set captures a high‑spirited performance featuring a beautiful segue into Comes A Time, Brent’s shimmering keys, and an inspiring Drums>Space. Recording quality is generally good for a soundboard source, though Midnight Hour sounds muddy and vocals can be echoey. The show swings between blinding brilliance and limp, uninspired passages, giving it a mixed but enjoyable character.

ai_rating.rationale: Most reviewers gave 4‑star ratings praising several songs and the overall soundboard clarity; only one reviewer was more critical, pulling the average down to a solid but not exceptional score.

recording_quality.technical_notes: Generally clean and balanced, but Midnight Hour is muddy and some vocals (e.g., Me & My Uncle) sound echoey. No major drop‑outs reported.

show_quality.setlist_flow: The band was in high spirits, delivering short‑sweet jams and smooth segues (notably into Comes A Time). While several moments shine, the performance is uneven, with some limp stretches that prevent it from being a classic.

band_member_comments.Jerry: Performance described as both ‘blinding brilliance’ in parts and ‘limp, uninspired’ at times; high spirits noted.
band_member_comments.Phil: Stands out on West L.A. Fadeaway and is highlighted during the band introduction (“Phil how do you do”).
band_member_comments.Bob: Introduces the band during Good Lovin; vocal harmonies are criticized as stepping on him; his intro attempts levity.
band_member_comments.Keys: Brent’s mid‑80s keyboard flair praised in Comes A Time; also referenced humorously as “that guy on keyboards.”
band_member_comments.Drums: Drums>Space is singled out as a highlight; Mickey and Bill are noted as the ‘drummers’ during the intro.
